narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings—pouring your heart into law school dreams only to have them paused like this feels deeply unfair, and I'm here to walk you through why our prediction model saw your path to the bar exam the way it did, like sharing the quiet details of a story that shaped its view. At the top of what stood out was your Year 1 decile of 3.0, which was the strongest signal pulling toward a tougher road ahead, as if that early footing in law school set a challenging rhythm from the start. Right behind it, your Year 3 decile of 1.0 amplified that concern even more, painting a picture of grades that dipped lower over time, making the model worry about building the steady momentum needed to conquer the bar. Your LSAT score of 36.0 added to this narrative, whispering that the raw analytical edge might not quite carry you through the exam's marathon demands. Even your upper-middle family income, while a solid foundation, nudged the outlook downward in the model's eyes, perhaps reflecting patterns where that bracket doesn't always translate to the extra push for bar success. Your undergraduate GPA of 3.0 layered on another subtle drag, suggesting the academic habits from undergrad might echo into law's high-stakes tests. Being white and female, along with planning full-time studies, wove into this story as quieter threads, each tilting toward higher failure risk based on the paths the model has seen before. With all these pieces—your deciles leading the charge, followed by LSAT, income, GPA, and the rest—coming together, the model landed at a 67% chance of not passing the bar, enough for us to hold back on admission to protect your time and dreams from that steep odds. It's not the full you, just a snapshot from these metrics, and many have rewritten their stories from here. What if you targeted bolstering those early grades or LSAT prep in a retake? Your passion could still light the way forward—keep going.
